title: Sentence Fragment Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: A sentence fragment is an incomplete sentence punctuated as if it were complete - it's usually missing a subject, a verb, or a complete thought. Here are 50 common fragments, explained simply, with the fix needed to make each one a complete, grammatical sentence.

commonMistakes.right: I went to bed because I was tired.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment starts with 'because', which makes it a dependent clause - it explains a reason but can't stand alone without a main sentence attached. Whenever a sentence begins with 'because', 'although', 'since', or 'when', make sure it's connected to a complete main clause.

commonMistakes.right: She was running through the park.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment has no clear subject or complete verb - 'running' needs a helping verb (was/is/were) and a subject to become a full sentence. Always check that your sentence has both someone/something doing the action AND a complete verb form.

commonMistakes.right: The book on the table is mine.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment only has a subject (the book) with a description (on the table), but no verb telling us what happens - it needs a verb to complete the thought. Ask yourself: what is happening, or what is being said about the subject? Add that missing verb.

commonMistakes.right: Although she studied hard, she didn't pass the exam.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'because', 'although' introduces a dependent clause that needs a main clause to complete the thought - it can't stand alone as a full sentence. Whenever you start with 'although' or 'even though', continue the sentence with what happened despite that condition.

commonMistakes.wrong: When I arrived at the station.
commonMistakes.right: When I arrived at the station, the train had already left.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment describes a time ('when I arrived') but doesn't tell us what happened at that time - it needs a main clause attached. Time clauses starting with 'when', 'while', or 'as soon as' always need to be connected to a complete sentence.

commonMistakes.right: She is a very talented singer.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment describes something but has no subject or verb showing who or what it's describing. Add a subject (she, he, the artist) and a verb (is, was) to complete the sentence.

commonMistakes.right: He is working late every night.
commonMistakes.why: Similar to the earlier example, this fragment has an action word ('working') but lacks a subject and helping verb to make it complete. Add 'he is', 'she was', or another subject plus helping verb before the action.

commonMistakes.wrong: Which is why I left early.
commonMistakes.right: I felt sick, which is why I left early.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment begins with 'which', referring back to an idea that isn't stated - it needs the first part of the sentence to make sense. Whenever you start a sentence with 'which', check that the thing 'which' refers to is already mentioned.

commonMistakes.right: He was standing alone in the rain.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment describes an action ('standing') without telling us who is doing it or providing a complete verb form. Add a subject and helping verb: 'he was standing', 'she is standing', to complete the thought.

commonMistakes.right: Even though it was late, we decided to continue working.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'although', the phrase 'even though' introduces a condition that needs a main clause to complete the meaning. This dependent clause pattern is one of the most common causes of sentence fragments, so watch for these starting words carefully.

commonMistakes.right: Since I have no money, I can't go shopping today.
commonMistakes.why: 'Since' here introduces a reason, making this a dependent clause that needs a main clause attached to form a complete sentence. Whenever 'since' means 'because' at the start of a sentence, remember it needs a full second part.

commonMistakes.wrong: The man wearing a blue shirt.
commonMistakes.right: The man wearing a blue shirt is my uncle.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment describes a person with detail (wearing a blue shirt) but never tells us what he does or what is said about him - a verb is missing. Complete the thought by adding a verb: is, was, walked, spoke, etc.

commonMistakes.wrong: If you have time this weekend.
commonMistakes.right: If you have time this weekend, let's meet for coffee.
commonMistakes.why: 'If' introduces a condition, and conditions always need a result attached to become complete - this fragment is missing that result. Whenever you start with 'if', make sure to finish the thought with what would happen under that condition.

commonMistakes.right: After finishing my homework, I watched a movie.
commonMistakes.why: 'After' introduces a time relationship, but this fragment doesn't say what happened after - it needs a main clause. Time words like 'after' and 'before' always need to connect to a complete action or event.

commonMistakes.right: Before you leave the house, please lock the door.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'after', 'before' sets up a time condition that needs to be finished with the actual instruction or event. This fragment leaves the reader wondering: before you leave, what should happen?

commonMistakes.wrong: A beautiful sunset over the ocean.
commonMistakes.right: We watched a beautiful sunset over the ocean.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment is just a descriptive phrase with no subject performing an action or verb showing what happened. Add who is experiencing or doing something with this sunset, plus a verb.

commonMistakes.right: While waiting for the bus, I read a book.
commonMistakes.why: 'While' introduces a time relationship for something happening simultaneously, and this fragment needs the second event attached. Complete it by adding what you were doing during that waiting time.

commonMistakes.right: Unless you apologize to her, she won't forgive you.
commonMistakes.why: 'Unless' sets up a condition (similar to 'if not'), and this fragment is missing the result of that condition. Always finish an 'unless' clause with what will or won't happen.

commonMistakes.right: He was trying his best to succeed.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment has an action ('trying') but no clear subject and helping verb attached to make it a complete sentence. Add a subject and 'was/is' before the -ing verb to complete it.

commonMistakes.right: Because of the heavy rain, the match was postponed.
commonMistakes.why: 'Because of' introduces a reason but this phrase alone doesn't tell us the RESULT of that reason - a main clause needs to follow. This is different from 'because' alone (a full clause) - 'because of' is followed by a noun phrase, then needs its own separate main clause.

commonMistakes.right: She won the competition, which made everyone very happy.
commonMistakes.why: Starting a sentence with 'which' referring to something not yet mentioned leaves the reader confused about what 'which' refers to. Always attach this kind of clause to the sentence describing the event it refers to.

commonMistakes.wrong: The girl who always arrives late.
commonMistakes.right: The girl who always arrives late missed the meeting today.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment describes a person in detail (who always arrives late) but never says what she actually did - the main verb for the sentence is missing. Complete the thought with what this person did or does.

commonMistakes.wrong: In order to save money for college.
commonMistakes.right: In order to save money for college, she took a part-time job.
commonMistakes.why: 'In order to' introduces a purpose, but the fragment doesn't say what action was taken for that purpose. Complete it by stating the action someone took to achieve that goal.

commonMistakes.wrong: As soon as the bell rings.
commonMistakes.right: As soon as the bell rings, the students will leave the classroom.
commonMistakes.why: 'As soon as' sets up a time condition, and this fragment is missing what happens at that moment. Always follow 'as soon as' with the resulting action.

commonMistakes.right: Despite trying really hard, he didn't win the race.
commonMistakes.why: 'Despite' introduces a contrast, and this fragment doesn't complete that contrast with what actually happened. Whenever you start with 'despite' or 'in spite of', finish with the surprising or contrasting outcome.

commonMistakes.wrong: So that everyone could hear clearly.
commonMistakes.right: She spoke loudly so that everyone could hear clearly.
commonMistakes.why: 'So that' introduces a purpose, but this fragment doesn't tell us the action taken for that purpose. Complete the sentence with what was done to achieve this goal.

commonMistakes.wrong: Considering how difficult the test was.
commonMistakes.right: Considering how difficult the test was, she did very well.
commonMistakes.why: 'Considering' introduces a factor to think about, but this fragment doesn't complete the thought about what happened given that factor. Add the main point or outcome you want to relate to this consideration.

commonMistakes.wrong: The team that won the championship last year.
commonMistakes.right: The team that won the championship last year is playing again tonight.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment describes a team in detail but never says what the team is doing now - the main verb is missing. Add a verb describing the team's current action or state.

commonMistakes.wrong: Provided that you finish on time.
commonMistakes.right: Provided that you finish on time, you can leave early.
commonMistakes.why: 'Provided that' sets up a condition (similar to 'if'), and needs a result attached to complete the sentence. Always follow conditional phrases like this with what will happen if the condition is met.

commonMistakes.right: Not knowing what to say, she stayed silent.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment describes a state ('not knowing') but doesn't tell us what the person did as a result. Complete it by adding what action followed from that uncertainty.

commonMistakes.right: Given the current circumstances, we need to postpone the event.
commonMistakes.why: 'Given' here introduces a condition or context, but this fragment needs a main clause to state the actual decision or outcome. Complete the thought with what should be done considering this context.

commonMistakes.right: Whatever you decide to do, I will support you.
commonMistakes.why: 'Whatever' introduces an open condition, and this fragment is missing the promise or statement connected to it. Complete it with what will happen regardless of the choice made.

commonMistakes.right: The reason for her absence was unclear.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment names a topic (the reason) but doesn't say anything about it - a verb is needed to complete the thought. Add a verb like 'was', 'remains', or 'is' to finish the sentence.

commonMistakes.right: To become a successful entrepreneur, you need patience and hard work.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment states a goal ('to become...') but doesn't say what's needed to achieve it - it's an incomplete infinitive phrase needing a main clause. Whenever a sentence starts with 'to + verb' describing a goal, complete it with what's required or what happens.

commonMistakes.right: Regardless of the outcome, we tried our best.
commonMistakes.why: 'Regardless of' introduces a condition being dismissed as unimportant, and this fragment needs the main point attached. Complete the thought with what remains true no matter the result.

commonMistakes.right: Having finished all my chores, I relaxed on the couch.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment describes a completed action ('having finished') but doesn't say what happened next - it needs a main clause. Whenever you start with 'having + past participle', follow it with what you did afterward.

commonMistakes.wrong: Which is something I've always wanted.
commonMistakes.right: I finally got the job, which is something I've always wanted.
commonMistakes.why: Starting with 'which' here refers to something not yet mentioned in this fragment, leaving the reader confused. Always attach this clause to the sentence describing what 'which' refers back to.

commonMistakes.wrong: As long as you try your best.
commonMistakes.right: As long as you try your best, that's all that matters.
commonMistakes.why: 'As long as' introduces a condition, and this fragment needs the result or consequence attached to complete the sentence. Complete conditional phrases like this with what follows from meeting the condition.

commonMistakes.wrong: Whether we like it or not.
commonMistakes.right: Whether we like it or not, we have to follow the rules.
commonMistakes.why: 'Whether...or not' introduces two possibilities, but this fragment doesn't state what remains true regardless of the choice. Complete it with the outcome that applies either way.

commonMistakes.right: Until the situation improves, we will continue working from home.
commonMistakes.why: 'Until' sets up a time limit, and this fragment needs the ongoing action attached to complete the thought. Whenever you use 'until' to mark an endpoint, state what continues happening before that point.

commonMistakes.wrong: Assuming everything goes according to plan.
commonMistakes.right: Assuming everything goes according to plan, we should finish by Friday.
commonMistakes.why: 'Assuming' introduces a hypothetical condition, and this fragment needs the expected outcome attached. Complete conditional phrases starting with 'assuming' by adding what you expect will happen.

commonMistakes.wrong: For the sake of the team's success.
commonMistakes.right: For the sake of the team's success, everyone worked overtime.
commonMistakes.why: 'For the sake of' introduces a purpose or motivation, but this fragment doesn't say what action was taken for that purpose. Complete it with the action people took motivated by this goal.

commonMistakes.wrong: The people who live next door.
commonMistakes.right: The people who live next door are very friendly.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment describes a group of people in detail but never says anything about them - the main verb is missing. Add a verb describing something about these people.

commonMistakes.right: According to the latest report, sales have increased by 20%.
commonMistakes.why: 'According to' introduces a source of information, and this fragment needs the actual information attached to complete the sentence. Whenever you cite a source with 'according to', follow it with what that source says.

commonMistakes.wrong: In spite of all the challenges we faced.
commonMistakes.right: In spite of all the challenges we faced, we completed the project.
commonMistakes.why: 'In spite of' introduces a contrast, similar to 'despite', and this fragment needs the surprising positive outcome attached. Complete contrast phrases like this with what happened anyway, despite the difficulty mentioned.

commonMistakes.right: As far as I know, the meeting is still scheduled for tomorrow.
commonMistakes.why: 'As far as I know' introduces a qualified statement of knowledge, and this fragment needs the actual information attached. Complete it with what you believe to be true, based on your limited knowledge.

commonMistakes.wrong: Once you understand the basic rules.
commonMistakes.right: Once you understand the basic rules, the game becomes much easier.
commonMistakes.why: 'Once' introduces a time condition (meaning 'after'), and this fragment needs the resulting situation attached. Complete time clauses starting with 'once' by adding what happens after that point.

commonMistakes.right: Whoever finishes first will receive a prize.
commonMistakes.why: 'Whoever' introduces an open subject referring to an unknown person, and this fragment needs a verb telling us what happens to that person. Complete it with the action or result that applies to whoever fits the description.

commonMistakes.wrong: Now that the exams are over.
commonMistakes.right: Now that the exams are over, we can finally relax.
commonMistakes.why: 'Now that' introduces a reason related to timing, and this fragment needs the resulting action or feeling attached. Complete time-related reason clauses like this with what can now happen.

commonMistakes.right: Seeing how tired she looked, I offered to help.
commonMistakes.why: This fragment describes an observation ('seeing how tired she looked') but doesn't say what action followed from that observation. Complete it with what you did in response to what you noticed.

quiz.question: Which of these is a complete sentence?
options: I went to bed because I was tired.
quiz.answer: I went to bed because I was tired.
quiz.explanation: This has a complete main clause attached to the dependent clause, making it a full sentence.

quiz.correction: Although she studied hard, she didn't pass the exam.
quiz.explanation: 'Although' introduces a dependent clause that needs a main clause to complete the thought.

quiz.question: What is a sentence fragment missing?
options: A subject, a verb, or a complete thought
quiz.answer: A subject, a verb, or a complete thought
quiz.explanation: Fragments are incomplete because they lack one of these essential parts.

quiz.question: Standing alone in the rain, ___ (he/waited) for the bus. (complete the fragment)
quiz.explanation: Adding a subject and verb completes the fragment into a full sentence.

quiz.question: Which word, when starting a sentence, often creates a fragment if not attached to a main clause?
quiz.explanation: 'Because' introduces a dependent clause that cannot stand alone.
